title: Part I: The Mistress of Ekeby
paragraphs: There is a story which used to be told in Värmland, about a parson who had been expelled from his living on account of his love of brandy.

He is supposed to have gone about the country for a whole winter, homeless and miserable, and yet, in spite of his wretchedness and degradation, he was such a fine-looking fellow and talked so agreeably that many a young girl lost her heart to him. But he himself did not fall in love with anyone; for he had met the Mistress of Ekeby, and after that there was no room in his heart for any other woman.
People called him Gösta Berling, the cavalier.

He came one December evening to Ekeby, the greatest ironworks in Värmland, in the time of its glory, when all the blast furnaces were working and there were banquets and dancing and music every night. The major's wife—the Mistress of Ekeby—was the most powerful woman in Värmland. She owned seven ironworks, and her word was law over the great estates and their households. She had taken in twelve pensioners, twelve gentlemen down on their luck, and given them free board and lodging at Ekeby, asking only that they should be merry and charming and fill her great house with music and life.

These were the cavaliers of Ekeby. They hunted and fished and sang and played. They drove the major's horses and drank the major's wine. And in the evenings they gathered in the great hall and made music, and the cavaliers' wing rang with fiddles and singing and laughter.
Gösta Berling came to this house on a winter evening, cold and ragged and hungry. The Mistress of Ekeby saw him standing at the gate in the snowstorm and called him in.
'Come in,' she said. 'We have room for one more.'
He came in and sat by the fire, and they gave him food and drink, and the warmth crept back into his hands and feet. And the major's wife looked at him with her clear eyes and saw what he was.
'You were a minister of God,' she said.
'I was,' he said.
'And they turned you out?'
'Yes.'
'On account of drink?'
'Yes.'
She was silent for a moment, looking at him.
'Will you stay here?' she said. 'You can be a cavalier. You can play and be merry. You can forget what you were.'
He looked up at her. She was a tall, strong woman, still handsome, with grey in her hair. Her eyes were direct and kind.
'Why would you take in a ruined man like me?' he said.
'Because I have taken in eleven others like you,' she said simply. 'Because Ekeby has room for one more.'
So Gösta Berling became the twelfth cavalier of Ekeby, and for a time he was the merriest of them all.

title: Part II: The Cavaliers
paragraphs: The cavaliers of Ekeby were remarkable men. There were twelve of them, and they were all gentlemen who had somehow come down in the world—a former judge, an officer who had lost his commission, an artist whose hands had grown unsteady, a pastor whose love of drink had been his ruin. They were failures, all of them, but at Ekeby they were at home.

Ekeby itself was like a kingdom. The major's wife ruled it with a firm hand. She saw to it that the furnaces burned and the iron was cast and the books were kept. But in the evenings the cavaliers ruled, and then Ekeby was a place of laughter and music and stories.

Gösta Berling was the finest of the cavaliers. He could play the violin, and when he played, people wept. He could speak, and when he spoke, people believed whatever he said. He was handsome and witty and full of life, and the women adored him. But he was restless and unhappy underneath it all, and only when he drank did the restlessness ease.

He had not always been like this. Once he had been a minister of God, standing in his pulpit and preaching with a tongue of fire. But then the drink had got hold of him, and one Sunday he had stood in the pulpit and could not speak—had stood there swaying, with the congregation watching, and finally had to be led away. And they had stripped him of his living and his calling, and he had gone out into the world a ruined man.
He was twenty-five years old when he came to Ekeby. He was still young, and the major's wife hoped that he might yet be saved.

But Värmland was a dangerous country for a man like Gösta. There was too much beauty there—too many dark forests and silver lakes and women with sad eyes—and there was too much wine. He rode through the countryside on the major's horses and was welcomed everywhere, and everywhere there was wine and music and women.

The young Countess Elisabet loved him. She was married to the young Count, but the Count was cold and correct and Gösta was alive. She looked at him when they danced and her cheeks flushed, and Gösta looked at her and his heart stirred, and they were both afraid.
The major's wife watched all this with knowing eyes.
'Be careful, Gösta,' she said to him one evening. 'You play with hearts the way a child plays with fire.'
'I know,' he said. 'I know.'
'Do you love her?'
He was silent for a while.
'I love them all,' he said at last. 'And none of them. That is my curse.'
The major's wife looked at him with something like sadness.
'One day,' she said, 'you will find the one you truly love. And then you will have to be worthy of her. Can you be worthy of her, Gösta?'
He did not answer.

title: Part III: The Great Year
paragraphs: There came a Christmas when everything at Ekeby seemed to be conspiring toward disaster. The major's wife had offended a powerful enemy—her own mother—and the enemy had struck back. There were lies and whispers and accusations, and suddenly the whole world seemed to be against the mistress of Ekeby.
And in her moment of weakness, the cavaliers did a terrible thing.

It happened at a Christmas feast. There had been too much wine and too much talk, and the cavaliers were in a reckless mood. And one of them—for a joke, or perhaps out of genuine recklessness—signed a document giving away Ekeby. A pact with the powers of darkness, some said. However it happened, the major's wife was driven from her own estate, and the cavaliers were its masters.
They celebrated their victory with a great feast. They drank and sang and believed they were free. But they had done a terrible thing, and the winter settled on them like a judgment.
Gösta Berling was at the center of all of it. He was the one who had driven the major's wife out into the cold, and he knew it, and the knowledge sat in him like a stone.
He went after her into the snowstorm.
'Come back,' he said. 'Come back to Ekeby.'
But she shook her head.
'Ekeby is yours now,' she said. 'Take care of it.'
And she went on into the storm.

The year that followed was strange and terrible. The cavaliers ruled Ekeby and were very bad at it. The furnaces went cold, the crops failed, the servants left. The cavaliers played and sang and tried to be merry, but their hearts were not in it.

And all through that year, Gösta Berling was changing. He fell in love—truly in love, for the first time—with a young Portuguese woman who had come to Värmland. Her name was Marianne Sinclaire, and she was the most beautiful woman he had ever seen. She was proud and cold at first, but Gösta could make anyone laugh, and little by little she softened.
But fate was against them. There was a fire, and Marianne's face was scarred, and she would not let Gösta see her. She sent him away.
'I love you,' he said.
'Go away,' she said. 'I am ruined. Let me go.'
'Your face is not you,' he said.
But she would not hear it. And he went away, as she asked.
He rode back through the winter forest, and the trees were dark and the snow was deep, and he thought about what love was and what it asked of a person.
He had not been worthy. He knew that now. The major's wife had asked him once: can you be worthy? And he had not answered. Now he knew the answer: not yet. Not yet.

title: Part IV: The Return
paragraphs: A year passed in this way—a year of failure and grief and slow change. The cavaliers could not hold Ekeby together, and one by one things fell apart. The iron trade suffered, the farms were neglected, and the great house grew quiet and cold.

Gösta Berling took to working with his hands. He went out into the fields and chopped wood and repaired fences and helped with the farm work. The other cavaliers laughed at him, but he paid them no mind. He was trying to learn something—trying to become someone who was worthy of more than just playing and drinking.

He met a young woman named Elisabet—a different Elisabet from the Countess—a simple country girl with clear eyes and a good heart. She did not know who he was, or what he had been. She knew only that he worked hard and was kind to animals and did not drink anymore.
'You are not like the other cavaliers,' she said to him one day.
'No,' he said. 'I am trying not to be.'
She looked at him with her clear eyes.
'What were you, before?' she asked.
He told her. He told her everything—the pastorate and the drink and the disgrace and the years at Ekeby and everything he had done wrong. He had never told anyone the whole truth before.
When he finished she was quiet for a while.
'That is a heavy thing to carry,' she said.
'Yes,' he said.
'But you are carrying it,' she said. 'You haven't put it down and run away.'
'Not anymore,' he said.
The major's wife came back to Ekeby at last—older, thinner, but with the same direct eyes. She stood at the gate and looked at the house, and Gösta came to meet her.
'You've let it go to ruin,' she said.
'Yes,' he said. 'I'm sorry.'
She looked at him.
'Are you different now?' she said.
'I think so,' he said. 'I don't know if I'm better. But I'm different.'
She nodded.
'Come,' she said. 'There is work to do.'
They went in together, and the work of rebuilding began.

Gösta Berling married Elisabet that spring, in a small ceremony in the country church. The cavaliers were there—all twelve of them—and they played music and sang, as they had always done. But there was something different about the music now. It was sweeter somehow, and a little sad.
The major's wife stood at the back of the church and watched, and she was smiling.

Värmland bloomed that spring—the forests were green and the lakes sparkled and the ironworks at Ekeby lit up their furnaces again. And Gösta Berling, who had been the most reckless of the cavaliers, became the most settled and the most content.

He had learned what love asks. It asks that you become worthy of it. It asks the work of years, and the putting away of easy pleasures, and the long slow patient labor of becoming a person who can be trusted with another person's heart.
He was thirty now, and he had learned it.
That was the saga of Gösta Berling—the cavalier who came in from the cold and, in the end, found his way home.
